listening.title: Drinks could go up in price by 50p under new return scheme, industry says
listening.topic: Wales plans a deposit return scheme for drinks containers in 2027, but industry warns including glass will raise prices and reduce consumer choice.
listening.topic_zh: 威爾士計劃於2027年推行飲料容器押金回收計劃，但業界警告將玻璃納入計劃將推高價格並減少消費者選擇。
listening.transcript_excerpt: Welcome to today's discussion on environmental policy and consumer economics. Across the United Kingdom, governments are rolling out deposit return schemes — systems where shoppers pay a small deposit when buying a drink in a bottle or can, and then get that money back when they return the empty container for recycling. The idea is straightforward: incentivise consumers to recycle rather than throw packaging away, reducing litter and boosting recycling rates. Scotland launched its own version of the scheme, and England is preparing to follow suit. Wales, however, has taken a slightly different approach. The Welsh government plans to include glass bottles in its deposit return scheme, which is scheduled to come into force on the first of October 2027. This decision has sparked significant controversy within the drinks industry. The British Soft Drinks Association argues that handling glass requires far more complex and expensive infrastructure than plastic or aluminium. Reverse vending machines capable of processing glass cost considerably more, and dedicated collection logistics must be put in place. Industry representatives warn that these additional costs will almost certainly be passed on to consumers, potentially adding as much as fifty pence to the price of certain bottled drinks. Some producers have even threatened to withdraw specific products from Welsh shelves altogether if the scheme proceeds as planned. The Welsh government, for its part, insists it remains committed to the scheme and has pushed back against what it calls misinformation being spread about its proposals. Environmental charity Keep Wales Tidy has also voiced frustration, arguing that the scheme is necessary to tackle persistent litter problems across the country.

vocabulary.definition: A government-backed recycling system in which consumers pay an upfront deposit on drinks containers and receive a refund upon returning them to a collection point.
vocabulary.definition_zh: 押金回收計劃：一種由政府支持的回收系統，消費者在購買飲料容器時預付押金，歸還空容器後可取回退款。
vocabulary.example: The deposit return scheme is expected to significantly increase recycling rates by giving consumers a financial incentive to return bottles and cans.

vocabulary.definition: An automated machine that accepts used drinks containers such as bottles and cans, scans them, and issues a refund or voucher to the user.
vocabulary.definition_zh: 逆向自動販賣機：一種自動化機器，接受用過的飲料容器如瓶子和罐子，掃描後向使用者發出退款或優惠券。
vocabulary.example: Supermarkets will need to install reverse vending machines to allow customers to return empty containers under the new scheme.

vocabulary.definition: The fundamental physical systems and facilities needed to support an operation or service, such as buildings, machinery, and transport networks.
vocabulary.definition_zh: 基礎設施：支持某項運作或服務所需的基本實體系統和設施，如建築物、機械和運輸網絡。
vocabulary.example: Including glass in the scheme requires dedicated collection infrastructure that is far more costly than that needed for plastic containers.

vocabulary.definition: To transfer additional business expenses to customers by raising prices rather than absorbing the costs internally.
vocabulary.definition_zh: 轉嫁成本：將額外的業務費用通過提高價格轉移給消費者，而非由公司內部承擔。
vocabulary.example: If the new regulations increase production expenses significantly, manufacturers are likely to pass on those costs to shoppers.

vocabulary.definition: False or inaccurate information, especially when spread deliberately or carelessly in a way that causes public confusion or harm.
vocabulary.definition_zh: 錯誤資訊：不正確或虛假的信息，尤其是以引起公眾混淆或造成傷害的方式刻意或粗心地傳播時。
vocabulary.example: The charity accused certain industry groups of spreading misinformation about the environmental impact of the proposed recycling policy.

questions.q: According to the episode, why is including glass considered the main problem with Wales' deposit return scheme?
options: A: Glass bottles are already recycled at very high rates in Wales
B: Glass requires more complex infrastructure and more expensive reverse vending machines
C: Glass containers are banned under existing UK environmental regulations
D: The Welsh government cannot afford to build any new collection points
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that glass is the main sticking point because including it makes the scheme significantly more complex and expensive, requiring dedicated collection infrastructure and more costly reverse vending machines.

questions.q: What do industry representatives say companies are likely to do in response to the additional costs imposed by the scheme?
options: A: Lobby the Welsh government to abandon recycling targets entirely
B: Relocate their manufacturing operations outside Wales
C: Pass costs on to shoppers or withdraw products from sale
D: Partner with environmental charities to subsidise the scheme
questions.explanation: Industry representatives warned that companies were likely to either pass the additional costs on to shoppers or withdraw products from sale altogether, both of which would negatively affect Welsh consumers.

questions.q: How does Wales' deposit return scheme differ from those being launched elsewhere in the UK?
options: A: Wales is launching its scheme five years earlier than the rest of the UK
B: Wales is the only region that will use reverse vending machines
C: Wales has chosen to include glass bottles, unlike other UK schemes
D: Wales will only accept plastic bottles and exclude aluminium cans
questions.explanation: The episode clearly states that schemes elsewhere in the UK are launching without glass to keep costs down and simplify cross-border systems, while Wales has chosen to include glass in its version of the scheme.

reading.title: Deposit Return Schemes: Balancing Environmental Goals with Economic Realities
reading.passage:
Deposit return schemes (DRS) represent one of the most widely discussed tools in modern environmental policy. By requiring consumers to pay a small surcharge on drinks containers at the point of purchase — a deposit that is subsequently refunded upon the return of the empty container — governments aim to create a direct financial incentive for recycling. Evidence from countries that have long operated such systems, including Germany, Norway, and several Canadian provinces, suggests that well-designed DRS can achieve container return rates exceeding 90 percent, dramatically reducing litter and easing pressure on conventional waste management infrastructure.

Despite these encouraging precedents, the implementation of deposit return schemes frequently generates significant controversy, particularly when policymakers must decide which materials to include. Plastic bottles and aluminium cans are generally considered straightforward to incorporate, as they are lightweight, standardised, and compatible with existing automated sorting technology. Glass, by contrast, presents a considerably greater logistical challenge. Its weight increases transportation costs, its fragility demands more robust handling procedures, and the reverse vending machines capable of processing glass containers require substantially greater capital investment than those designed exclusively for plastic and metal.

These practical concerns have led most jurisdictions introducing new DRS to initially exclude glass, prioritising simplicity and cross-border consistency. Wales, however, has diverged from this approach by proposing to include glass in its scheme, which is scheduled to launch in October 2027. Industry stakeholders have responded with alarm, arguing that the added complexity will drive up retail prices by as much as fifty pence per bottle and may prompt some producers to withdraw certain products from the Welsh market entirely. Consumer advocacy groups and environmental organisations find themselves divided, with some prioritising the environmental benefits of comprehensive glass recycling and others expressing concern about the regressive impact of higher prices on lower-income households. The Welsh government has defended its position, arguing that the long-term environmental benefits justify the transitional costs, while committing to review implementation details before the scheme goes live.

questions.statement: Germany and Norway are cited as examples of countries where deposit return schemes have achieved container return rates above 90 percent.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly mentions Germany, Norway, and several Canadian provinces as countries with long-established DRS that can achieve return rates exceeding 90 percent, confirming this statement.

questions.statement: The Welsh government has agreed to exclude glass from its deposit return scheme following pressure from the drinks industry.
questions.explanation: The passage states the opposite — Wales has diverged from other UK regions by proposing to include glass, and the Welsh government has defended this position, committing to review implementation details rather than abandoning glass inclusion.

questions.statement: Consumer advocacy groups are unanimously in favour of including glass in Wales' deposit return scheme.
questions.explanation: The passage states that consumer advocacy groups and environmental organisations are divided on the issue, with some concerned about the regressive impact of higher prices on lower-income households, so there is no unanimous support.

questions.q: According to the passage, which of the following best explains why most new deposit return schemes exclude glass?
options: A: Glass containers are already recycled through separate kerbside collection programmes
B: Glass inclusion increases costs, logistical complexity, and incompatibility with standard automated machines
C: Consumer demand for glass-bottled drinks has declined sharply in recent years
D: International trade agreements restrict the inclusion of glass in national recycling schemes
questions.explanation: The passage outlines multiple reasons why glass is problematic: its weight raises transport costs, its fragility demands more robust handling, and the required reverse vending machines need substantially greater capital investment. These factors combine to make glass inclusion costly and complex, which is why most jurisdictions initially exclude it.

speaking.topic: Describe an environmental policy or scheme that you think would benefit your community.
speaking.topic_zh: 描述一項你認為對你的社區有益的環保政策或計劃。

part2.cue_card:
Describe an environmental policy or scheme that you think would benefit your community.

You should say:
• what the policy or scheme is
• how it would work in practice
• what benefits it would bring to people and the environment

And explain why you think this particular policy is the most important one for your community right now.
band_tips: Use precise environmental and economic vocabulary such as 'incentivise', 'infrastructure', 'deposit return scheme', and 'carbon footprint' to demonstrate lexical range and push towards Band 8-9.

Organise your response with a clear narrative arc: briefly introduce the scheme, explain its mechanics, discuss multiple layers of benefit (environmental, economic, social), and conclude with a personal evaluative statement to show coherent reasoning.

Incorporate speculative and conditional language naturally — for example, 'were such a scheme to be introduced', 'this could potentially', 'one might argue' — to demonstrate grammatical range and avoid a monotonous declarative tone.

part3.q: Do you think financial incentives are more effective than fines and penalties in encouraging people to recycle?
part3.q_zh: 你認為財務獎勵在鼓勵人們回收方面比罰款和處罰更有效嗎？
key_points: Financial incentives create positive associations with desired behaviour, making compliance feel rewarding rather than coercive, which tends to produce more sustainable long-term behavioural change.
Penalties can be effective deterrents but may generate public resentment, particularly when enforcement is perceived as inconsistent or when fines fall disproportionately on lower-income households.
A combined approach — rewarding compliance while penalising persistent non-compliance — may represent the most pragmatic policy design, as seen in successful DRS models in Scandinavia.

part3.q: Should businesses or governments bear the primary responsibility for reducing packaging waste?
part3.q_zh: 企業還是政府應該承擔減少包裝廢物的主要責任？
key_points: Governments possess the regulatory authority to set mandatory standards and create system-wide frameworks that individual businesses acting alone cannot achieve, making state-level intervention essential for structural change.

Businesses have both the technical expertise and the market incentive to develop innovative packaging solutions, particularly as consumer demand for sustainable products grows, suggesting corporate responsibility is equally critical.

The most effective outcomes typically arise from co-regulatory models in which governments establish minimum standards and businesses compete to exceed them, sharing responsibility rather than assigning it exclusively to one actor.

part3.q: How might a deposit return scheme affect people on low incomes differently from wealthier consumers?
part3.q_zh: 押金回收計劃可能會對低收入人群和富裕消費者產生怎樣不同的影響？
key_points: For lower-income households, the upfront deposit represents a proportionally larger financial burden, effectively acting as a regressive tax if they struggle to access return points conveniently or consistently.

Conversely, lower-income consumers who diligently return containers may benefit more meaningfully from the refunds relative to their disposable income, potentially making the scheme marginally progressive in practice.

Policymakers can mitigate regressive impacts by ensuring equitable geographic distribution of return points, particularly in rural and economically deprived areas where access to infrastructure tends to be limited.

part3.q: To what extent should environmental concerns take priority over economic considerations when governments design new policies?
part3.q_zh: 在政府設計新政策時，環境考量應在多大程度上優先於經濟因素？
key_points: Short-term economic costs must be weighed against the long-term economic consequences of environmental degradation, including costs associated with climate change, resource depletion, and public health impacts, which often dwarf initial policy investment.

Democratic accountability means governments must balance environmental idealism with the lived economic realities of citizens, particularly during periods of inflation or economic hardship when public tolerance for price increases is low.

A false dichotomy exists between environmental and economic priorities in many cases, as well-designed green policies — such as circular economy initiatives — can simultaneously generate economic value, create employment, and reduce environmental harm.

writing.task2_question: Some people believe that governments should use financial incentives, such as deposit return schemes, to encourage environmentally responsible consumer behaviour. Others argue that such measures unfairly increase the cost of living and that businesses, not consumers, should be held responsible for reducing packaging waste. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
writing.task2_question_zh: 一些人認為政府應使用財務激勵措施（如押金回收計劃）來鼓勵環保的消費者行為。另一些人認為這類措施不公平地增加了生活成本，應由企業而非消費者承擔減少包裝廢物的責任。討論兩種觀點並給出你自己的意見。

useful_phrases.phrase: proponents of this view contend that
useful_phrases.when_to_use: Use when introducing the supporting arguments for one side of a debate before presenting the counterargument or your own position.
useful_phrases.when_to_use_zh: 在呈現反駁論點或個人立場之前，介紹辯論某一方的支持論點時使用。
useful_phrases.example: Proponents of this view contend that financial incentives are among the most efficient mechanisms available to governments seeking to modify consumer behaviour at scale.

useful_phrases.phrase: a more nuanced perspective suggests that
useful_phrases.when_to_use: Use when moving beyond binary positions to introduce a qualified or complex analysis that acknowledges multiple factors simultaneously.
useful_phrases.when_to_use_zh: 當超越二元立場，引入承認多重因素的複雜分析時使用。
useful_phrases.example: A more nuanced perspective suggests that the effectiveness of deposit return schemes depends heavily on the accessibility of collection infrastructure across different socioeconomic groups.

useful_phrases.when_to_use: Use to concede a valid point from the opposing argument before reinforcing your own stance, demonstrating balanced reasoning.
useful_phrases.when_to_use_zh: 在強調自己立場之前，承認對方論點的合理性時使用，以展示平衡的推理能力。
useful_phrases.example: It is worth acknowledging that for lower-income households, mandatory deposits on everyday beverages can represent a disproportionate financial burden.

useful_phrases.phrase: the evidence suggests that on balance
useful_phrases.when_to_use: Use in the conclusion or when summarising your overall judgement after weighing competing arguments throughout the essay.
useful_phrases.when_to_use_zh: 在整篇文章中權衡相互競爭的論點後，在結論或總結整體判斷時使用。
useful_phrases.example: The evidence suggests that on balance, shared responsibility between governments, businesses, and consumers produces the most effective and equitable environmental outcomes.

useful_phrases.when_to_use: Use to challenge the premise that two ideas are contradictory, arguing instead that they can coexist or be complementary.
useful_phrases.when_to_use_zh: 用於挑戰兩個想法相互矛盾的前提，反而主張它們可以共存或互補時使用。
useful_phrases.example: Environmental protection and economic growth are far from being mutually exclusive; indeed, well-designed green policies have repeatedly demonstrated the capacity to stimulate innovation and job creation simultaneously.

outline.intro: Begin by paraphrasing the debate between consumer-targeted financial incentives and corporate responsibility for packaging waste. Briefly acknowledge that both perspectives reflect legitimate concerns before clearly stating your own position — ideally that a co-regulatory approach combining both is most effective.
outline.body1: Examine the case for government-administered financial incentives such as DRS. Argue that they create direct, transparent behavioural nudges, have demonstrated high success rates internationally (e.g., Germany, Norway), and generate revenue that can fund recycling infrastructure. Acknowledge the concern about cost-of-living impacts but note that well-designed schemes refund deposits, meaning disciplined consumers do not ultimately pay more.
outline.body2: Present the argument that businesses bear greater responsibility, given that they design packaging, control supply chains, and have the technical capacity to innovate. Discuss how producer responsibility schemes — which hold manufacturers financially accountable for end-of-life packaging costs — can drive systemic change without burdening consumers. However, concede that without government-mandated frameworks, voluntary corporate action tends to be insufficient and inconsistent.
outline.conclusion: Restate your position that the consumer-versus-business framing is a false dichotomy. The most sustainable environmental policy outcomes arise when governments set binding standards, businesses innovate to meet and exceed them, and consumer incentives reinforce desired behaviours throughout the system. Emphasise that equity considerations — particularly protecting lower-income groups — must be central to any scheme design.
